text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Gynecologic Oncology

PATIENT: Marguerite Okonkwo
ATTENDING: [Gynecologic Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Routine Surveillance / Maintenance Therapy Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 49-year-old Nigerian-American woman with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC founder mutation), presenting today for her 24-month surveillance visit. She is currently on Zejula maintenance therapy (100 mg orally once daily, individualized dose) and is 18 months into her planned 36-month maintenance course. She presents for routine clinical and laboratory reassessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed 24 months ago with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3cN1M0), with diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, and bulky pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy. Her initial serum CA-125 was markedly elevated at 4,872 U/mL. She underwent primary debulking surgery (PDS) via midline laparotomy approximately one month after diagnosis, which included bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, sigmoid mesenteric implant resection, and right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping. Complete gross resection (R0) was achieved with no macroscopic residual disease. Final pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) at the fimbrial ends of both fallopian tubes, 4 of 18 right pelvic nodes positive, 2 of 12 para-aortic infrarenal nodes positive, all margins negative.

Germline testing revealed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, Class 5 pathogenic), and somatic tumor profiling (Foundation One CDx) showed TP53 p.R175H, BRCA1 somatic LOH, RB1 loss, CCNE1 amplification, and an HRD score of 62 (above threshold, HRD positive). TMB was 6.2 mut/Mb and MSS.

Six weeks post-operatively, she began adjuvant carbo AUC 6 / Taxol 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days. She completed all 6 cycles, with the following notable toxicities: Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (stocking-glove distribution, managed conservatively without dose reduction), and a Grade 2 Paraplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 4 (managed with extended desensitization protocol for cycles 5-6 without further events). She achieved CA-125 normalization (11 U/mL) and a complete radiographic response by end-of-treatment restaging imaging.

Approximately 8 months after diagnosis, she initiated Zejula maintenance therapy. Given her body weight of 63 kg (below the 77 kg threshold), she was started on the individualized starting dose of 200 mg daily per FDA label. Approximately 4 weeks into maintenance, she developed Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir of 98,000/mcL), requiring a 28-day hold with subsequent dose reduction to 100 mg daily upon resumption. She has remained on Zejula 100 mg daily since then, now 18 months into maintenance therapy. Hematologic parameters have been stable over the past year on the reduced dose. She also developed concurrent iron deficiency anemia (Grade 1, Hgb nadir 9.8 g/dL) attributed to surgical blood loss, chemotherapy-related erythropoietic suppression, and PARP inhibitor effect; this was managed with oral ferrous sulfate supplementation with subsequent hemoglobin improvement to the 11-11.8 g/dL range.

Surveillance imaging at 6 months and 12 months into maintenance (14 and 18 months from diagnosis) showed no evidence of disease. Annual CT at 24 months from diagnosis was performed in advance of today's visit.

Today, Ms. Okonkwo presents reporting excellent quality of life. She has returned to full-time work as a high school principal and is highly functional. She denies any symptoms suggestive of disease recurrence. Specifically, she denies new or progressive abdominal distension, new pelvic pressure or pain, early satiety, bloating, new GI complaints including constipation or change in bowel habits, dysuria, urinary frequency, or lower extremity edema. She denies shortness of breath, cough, or pleuritic chest pain. She denies unintentional weight loss. She reports continued mild vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes 2-3 times per day) from surgical menopause, which are manageable and improved compared to the early post-operative period. She continues escitalopram 10 mg daily, which she finds helpful for both mood and vasomotor symptom management. She reports mild vaginal dryness managed with over-the-counter vaginal moisturizer (Replens), which she applies 3 times per week with adequate relief. She denies vaginal bleeding.

Regarding Zejula tolerability: She takes nira at bedtime with ondansetron 8 mg approximately 30 minutes prior. Nausea has fully resolved and she no longer routinely requires the ondansetron, though she keeps it on hand. She reports no fatigue beyond what she considers her baseline. No new bruising or bleeding episodes. No hair loss (complete hair regrowth since completing chemotherapy). Peripheral neuropathy, which was Grade 2 at its peak during chemotherapy, has fully resolved; she reports no numbness, tingling, or sensory disturbance in her hands or feet.

Family history update: Her older child (age 22) was found to carry the same BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant and has been referred for enhanced surveillance per NCCN HBOC guidelines (annual breast MRI beginning at age 25, clinical breast exam semi-annually, risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy discussed for age 35-40). Her younger child (age 19) tested negative for the familial BRCA1 variant. Ms. Okonkwo has processed this information with the support of the oncology social worker and her family, and continues to advocate actively within her community around hereditary cancer awareness.

She raises a question today about whether she might discontinue nira before the planned 36-month endpoint, given her sense of wellbeing and curiosity about the rationale for the full duration.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats. Denies unintentional weight loss. Stable appetite. Energy good.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headache. No visual changes. No epistaxis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations. Known mild hypertension, stable on amlodipine.
Respiratory: No dyspnea, no cough, no pleuritic pain.
Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain. No nausea or vomiting at this time. No early satiety. No bloating or distension. Bowel habits regular. No rectal bleeding.
Genitourinary: No dysuria. No hematuria. No urinary frequency or urgency. No vaginal bleeding. Vaginal dryness as noted above, managed adequately.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias, no myalgias. No back pain.
Neurological: Peripheral neuropathy fully resolved (Grade 0). No headaches or focal deficits.
Hematologic: No bruising or bleeding. No petechiae.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Hypothyroidism managed on levothyroxine (stable). Surgical menopause symptoms as described.
Psychiatric: Mood good. Functioning well at work and home. Ongoing support from social work as needed. No depression or significant anxiety reported today.
Skin: Hair fully regrown. No skin rash or changes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.7 degrees C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight: 64.5 kg (stable, +1.5 kg from last visit). BMI: 23.8 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Affect bright and engaged. Wearing a natural hair style (full regrowth following chemotherapy-related alopecia).

Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. Conjunctivae non-pale. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No accessory muscle use.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Midline vertical laparotomy scar well-healed, non-tender. No palpable organomegaly. No ascites clinically (no shifting dullness, no fluid wave). No palpable masses.

Pelvic (limited external exam): External genitalia without lesions. Vaginal vault not formally examined at patient's preference today (last formal pelvic exam 3 months ago was unremarkable). No tenderness on light palpation of the lower abdomen.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness. Pulses intact.

Neurological: Cranial nerves intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation to light touch intact in bilateral upper and lower extremities (including fingertips and toes). No distal sensory deficit on monofilament or vibration testing. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Gait normal.

Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or ecchymoses. No jaundice. No rash.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 5.3 x10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC: 3.1 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild, stable; improved from nadir of 9.8 g/dL; iron supplementation ongoing)
  - MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)
  - Platelets: 181,000/mcL (normal; recovering from prior nira-related thrombocytopenia nadir, stable for 12+ months on 100 mg dose)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride: 103 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60, stable)
  - Glucose: 92 mg/dL
  - AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 18 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL (LFTs normal)
  - Albumin: 4.0 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
  - CA-125: 12 U/mL (within normal limits, reference <35 U/mL; stable over the past three surveillance visits: 8, 9, 10, 12 U/mL sequential values)
  - HE4: 52 pmol/L (normal for age and menopausal status)

Thyroid Function (annual check in setting of known hypothyroidism):
  - TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (well-controlled on levothyroxine)

Iron Studies (follow-up on prior iron deficiency):
  - Ferritin: 28 ng/mL (improved from 12 ng/mL; still low-normal, continue supplementation)
  - Serum iron: 68 mcg/dL (improving)
  - TIBC: 360 mcg/dL
  - Transferrin saturation: 19% (borderline, continue oral iron)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Annual surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast and CT chest (obtained prior to today's visit, read by staff radiologist, reviewed by me):

No evidence of disease. Post-surgical and post-treatment changes are stable, consistent with prior debulking surgery findings. No ascites. No peritoneal implants or omental nodularity. No pathologically enlarged pelvic or retroperitoneal lymph nodes. No hepatic lesions. No adnexal masses (bilateral oophorectomy status). CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Findings are unchanged from prior surveillance imaging. Durable complete response at 24-month surveillance.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), CURRENTLY IN COMPLETE REMISSION - 24 months from diagnosis, 18 months into nira maintenance therapy. CA-125 stable at 12 U/mL. Annual CT demonstrates no evidence of recurrent disease. Clinical examination without findings suggestive of recurrence.

2. Zejula maintenance therapy (PARP inhibitor, 100 mg orally daily) - well tolerated at individualized reduced dose. Hematologic parameters stable. No new toxicities. 18 of planned 36 months completed.

3. Hematologic: Mild persistent anemia (Hgb 11.8 g/dL), stable and improving. Iron deficiency partially corrected on oral ferrous sulfate; continuing supplementation. Thrombocytopenia fully resolved on reduced Zejula dose.

4. Surgical menopause - vasomotor symptoms and vaginal dryness managed non-hormonally with escitalopram and vaginal moisturizer. Stable.

5. Osteopenia (T-score -1.4 on most recent DXA) - on calcium + vitamin D. No bisphosphonate indicated at this time.

6. Hypothyroidism - well controlled on levothyroxine (TSH 2.1). Continue current dose.

7. Hypertension - controlled on amlodipine (BP 128/78 today). Stable.

8. BRCA1 hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome - family cascade testing completed; older child BRCA1 positive and enrolled in enhanced surveillance program; younger child BRCA1 negative.

9. Patient inquiry regarding early nira discontinuation - addressed in discussion below.

---

PLAN

1. Ovarian carcinoma surveillance and Zejula maintenance: Continue Zejula 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime. The patient asked about early discontinuation given her excellent quality of life and sense of wellbeing. I reviewed with her the evidence supporting the full 36-month maintenance duration, specifically the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial results demonstrating a statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in progression-free survival for patients completing the full maintenance course, particularly those who are HRD positive and/or BRCA mutated, as she is. I explained that the data from long-term follow-up (including secondary analyses) continue to support the planned duration, and that stopping early would sacrifice a portion of the durable benefit. She understands and agrees to complete the full 36-month course, barring toxicity or progression. I also briefly referenced the supportive data from SOLO-1 (Lynparza in BRCA-mutated HGSOC in first-line maintenance) as further context for the rationale for prolonged maintenance in this setting. She will continue Zejula through approximately 18 more months to complete the 36-month course.

2. Laboratory monitoring: CBC with differential every 4 weeks (stable protocol on nira 100 mg; platelet counts have been reassuring for >12 months - will continue q4 week monitoring per label). CA-125 every 3 months. CMP and LFTs at next visit. TSH annually.

3. Iron deficiency anemia: Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO BID with food. Recheck iron studies and CBC in 6-8 weeks. Goal ferritin >50 ng/mL. If hemoglobin fails to improve further or ferritin remains low despite supplementation, will consider IV iron infusion (e.g., ferric carboxymaltose) at that time.

4. Bone health: Continue calcium carbonate 600 mg PO BID and vitamin D3 1000 IU daily. Repeat DXA scan at 36 months from diagnosis (i.e., approximately 12 months from now). If T-score progresses to -2.5 or below (osteoporosis threshold), will initiate bisphosphonate therapy.

5. Surgical menopause management: Continue escitalopram 10 mg daily for vasomotor symptoms and mood support. Continue vaginal moisturizer PRN. No change to current regimen. Encouraged pelvic floor physical therapy if vaginal dryness or pelvic symptoms worsen.

6. Hypertension: Continue amlodipine at current dose. Blood pressure well controlled today. No adjustment needed.

7. Hypothyroidism: Continue levothyroxine at current dose (TSH well controlled). Annual TSH check.

8. Hereditary BRCA1 follow-up: Continue to follow her older child's referral to HBOC high-risk program. Remind patient that she herself does not require additional risk-reduction surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy already performed as part of PDS). Continue to offer genetic counseling support for family as needed.

9. Psychosocial: Patient continues to cope well. She is highly functional, working full time, and engaged in her community. She continues to have access to oncology social work for any emerging needs. Quality of life discussion reflects no significant unmet needs at this visit.

10. Next appointments: Gynecologic oncology clinic in 3 months for routine surveillance and CBC review. Repeat annual CT abdomen/pelvis and chest in approximately 12 months (at 36-month mark from diagnosis, which will also coincide with anticipated Zejula completion).

11. Return precautions: Patient instructed to contact the clinic promptly if she develops new or worsening abdominal distension, new pelvic pain, early satiety, significant bloating, unexplained weight loss, dyspnea, or any symptoms that concern her as potentially related to disease recurrence. She is provided with direct contact information for the on-call GYN oncology nurse and physician.

Time spent with patient: Approximately 40 minutes, more than 50% of which was devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Gynecologic Oncologist]
